## TauriAppTransport class

Carries the App API over Tauri IPC into a Holochain conductor running in the same process, instead of over a websocket.

From [AppWebsocket](./client.appwebsocket.md)<!-- -->'s perspective this is a drop-in for [WsClient](./client.wsclient.md)<!-- -->: it exposes the same `request` method and is an Emittery that emits `signal` events, so every AppWebsocket method works unchanged. Only the pipe differs — the same msgpack-encoded `{ type, value }` payloads a websocket would carry are sent through the `plugin:<name>|app_request` command. No app id is sent: the conductor scopes each request to the calling window on the Rust side, which replaces the per-app websocket auth token.

**Signature:**

```typescript
export declare class TauriAppTransport extends Emittery implements AppClientTransport 
```
**Extends:** Emittery

**Implements:** [AppClientTransport](./client.appclienttransport.md)

<tbody><tr><td>

[close()](./client.tauriapptransport.close.md)


</td><td>


</td><td>

Stop receiving signals.

Not reachable through [AppClientTransport](./client.appclienttransport.md) (the public transport surface is `request` + `on`<!-- -->), and [AppWebsocket](./client.appwebsocket.md) exposes no app-level disconnect — same as the websocket path. In practice the subscription lives for the lifetime of the webview. This is kept for explicit teardown in tests and any future wiring of a disconnect through the interface.


</td></tr>
<tr><td>

[request(request)](./client.tauriapptransport.request.md)


</td><td>


</td><td>

Send a tagged App API request through Tauri IPC and return the tagged response. The bytes on the wire are identical to the websocket path.


</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
